}";Orig;research contribution;"Greybox fuzzing is one of the most popular methods for detecting software vulnerabilities
column2: which conducts a biased random search within the program input space. To enhance its effectiveness in achieving deep coverage of program behaviors
column3: greybox fuzzing is often combined with concolic execution
column4: which performs a path-sensitive search over the domain of program inputs. In hybrid fuzzing
column5: conventional greybox fuzzing is followed by concolic execution in an iterative loop
column6: where reachability roadblocks encountered by greybox fuzzing are tackled by concolic execution. However
column7: such hybrid fuzzing still suffers from difficulties conventionally faced by symbolic execution
column8: such as the need for environment modeling and system call support. In this work
column9: we show how to achieve the effect of concolic execution without having to compute and solve symbolic path constraints. When coverage-based greybox fuzzing reaches a roadblock in terms of reaching certain branches
column10: we conduct a slicing on the execution trace and suggest modifications of the input to reach the relevant branches. A Large Language Model (LLM) is used as a solver to generate the modified input for reaching the desired branches. Compared with both the vanilla greybox fuzzer AFL and hybrid fuzzers Intriguer and Qsym
column11: our LLM-based hybrid fuzzer HyLLfuzz (pronounced ""hill fuzz"") demonstrates superior coverage. Furthermore
column12: the LLM-based concolic execution in HyLLfuzz takes a time that is 4-19 times faster than the concolic execution running in existing hybrid fuzzing tools. This experience shows that LLMs can be effectively inserted into the iterative loop of hybrid fuzzers
column13: to efficiently expose more program behaviors.";Test Augmentation or Improvement;Hybrid Prompting;Alone;Tool/Approach;Functional;;Other;GPT-Family;Test Coverage

column13: pages={122–127} };IEEE;;"The core challenge in autonomous driving decision-making systems lies in ensuring safety and robustness in complex and adversarial scenarios. Traditional reinforcement learning methods lack interpretability
column14: while recent approaches that use Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate interpretable rules

column17: we propose BladeRunner-AD

column19: while a ""stylized"" Adversarial Attacker agent analyzes the current policy’s logical flaws and generates adversarial scenarios in simulation most likely to cause it to fail. Through a closed loop of generation-attack-reflection-revision
column20: the policy co-evolves under continuous
column21: worst-case pressure. This mechanism
column22: akin to automated red teaming

column24: thereby achieving high robustness. We conducted comprehensive experiments in interactive scenarios on the highway-env simulation platform. and there is insufficient tool support to automate that procedure.
